content:
I asked: “what happened in 1974?”
The answer was in my document. Hawking radiation, 1974. The LLM said: “I don’t know.”
First step: don’t blame the LLM. Debug retrieval.
docs = retriever.invoke(“what happened in 1974”)
print(docs)
Output: four copies of the same intro paragraph.
Root cause: Every server restart ran Chroma.from_texts again with no count check. Same 10 chunks added on top — Run 1: 10 chunks, Run 2: 20 chunks, Run 3: 30 chunks. The intro paragraph dominated the vector space. All 4 retrieval slots went to it.
Why the intro ranked higher than the Hawking chunk: Vector search matches meaning, not keywords. “What happened in 1974?” semantically scores closer to “historical framing + dates” (the intro) than “quantum mechanics + thermal radiation” (the Hawking
chunk).
The LLM received:
“A black hole is a region of space…” (copy 1)
“A black hole is a region of space…” (copy 2)
“A black hole is a region of space…” (copy 3)
“A black hole is a region of space…” (copy 4)
Question: what happened in 1974?
It correctly said “I don’t know.” The bug was in retrieval, not the LLM.

Check before embedding. Embeddings run once. Every restart loads from disk.
Core lesson: the LLM is only as good as what the retriever gives it.

I asked “who is Stephen Hawking?” — got back: “Stephen Hawking.”
Prompt said “use only the context below.” Context only mentioned his name in passing. LLM obeyed.
Changed one thing:
# Before:
“Use only the context below. If not in context, say I don’t know.”
# After:
“Use the context below. If context isn’t enough, use your own knowledge but mention it.”
Same model. Same retriever. Completely different output.
One sentence in your prompt has more impact than switching to a bigger model.
